title: Matrix Points on Varieties
speaker: Asvin Gothandaraman
abstract:
We study a non-commutative counterpart Cn(X) of the symmetric product SymnX of a space X, defined as the space of nxn-matrix valued points on X. Our main result will be a precise formula for the cohomology of this space in great generality, but we see this as the first step in a wider non-commutative world. In particular, there is an appealing analogy with Weil restriction that we hope is illuminating and a taste of possible future directions. 
This is joint work with Yifeng Huang, Ruofan Jiang and Yifan Wei (who is on the job market and drove much of this current research!).
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Matrix points as noncommutative zero-cycles
proof_stages.summary: The talk recasts Sym^n(X) as parametrizing commutative algebra-valued zero-cycles and defines C_n(X) to represent O_X-algebra maps into M_n, equivalently framed finite flat sheaves, as a noncommutative analogue.

proof_stages.label: Finite-field zeta series and local point-count factors
proof_stages.summary: For varieties over finite fields, the speaker defines a series using |C_n(X)(F_q)|/|GL_n(F_q)|, obtains an Euler product over closed points with nilpotent local factors, explains the smooth-curve product formula, and notes the rapid complication in dimensions at least two.

proof_stages.label: Cohomological comparison theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem identifies the singular or etale cohomology of C_n(X) with that of (X^n x GL_n/T_n)/S_n under the stated hypotheses, using the interpretation of GL_n/T_n as decompositions of k^n into lines and Borel's presentation of its cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Semisimple comparison map over Sym^n(X)
proof_stages.summary: The speaker constructs S_n^X=(X^n x GL_n/T_n)/S_n and a natural map sigma:S_n^X -> C_n(X) by placing skyscraper sheaves or eigenspaces at the chosen points, then compares both sides over Sym^n(X).

proof_stages.label: Reduction to fiber cohomology by base change
proof_stages.summary: To prove the cohomological comparison, the argument first shows that the derived pushforwards along the two maps to Sym^n(X) can be checked on stalks as the cohomology of fibers, despite the maps not being proper.

proof_stages.label: Fiber comparison via contractibility and affine bundles
proof_stages.summary: For a cycle z=sum lambda_i x_i, the p_1-fiber is GL_n/T_n modulo the stabilizer S_lambda, while the p_2-fiber is built from local nilpotent matrix loci and GL-quotients; graded or G_m actions contract the nilpotent pieces and the remaining affine-bundle comparison has cohomologically trivial fibers.

proof_stages.label: Curve case and compactly supported cohomology
proof_stages.summary: When X is a curve, adding a compatible flag gives a space mapping to X^n with affine upper-triangular fibers, allowing compactly supported cohomology to be recovered from ordinary cohomology up to duality and twist, thereby linking the theorem back to the point-count product formula.

examples.name: Affine space as commuting matrix tuples
examples.note: For X=A^r, C_n(X) is identified with the scheme of r-tuples of pairwise commuting n by n matrices, making the definition of matrix points concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the definition to affine space, deriving that C_n(A^r) is the scheme of r commuting n-by-n matrices cut out inside M_n^r by commutator equations

passes.reason: Affine space is the maximally regular polynomial-algebra case, used only to illustrate matrix points as commuting tuples.
The example is the standard matrix-point scheme C_n(A^r) of pairwise commuting r-tuples, used illustratively rather than as a special boundary or obstruction.
Affine space is a homogeneous maximally regular variety, used here only to illustrate that matrix points are commuting tuples.
Affine space is the maximally regular affine model, used only to identify C_n(A^r) with commuting r-tuples of matrices.

affine space is used as a highly regular homogeneous test variety whose matrix points are commuting tuples, not as a failure case

Used as the basic illustration that C_n(A^r) is the commuting variety of r matrix tuples, with no adversarial role or sporadic/extreme structure.

examples.name: Multiplicative group as invertible matrices
examples.note: For X=G_m, a map k[t,t^{-1}] to M_n is the same as choosing an invertible matrix, illustrating how familiar matrix varieties arise from C_n(X).
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the construction to G_m by mapping k[t,t^{-1}] into M_n and identifies this concretely as choosing an invertible matrix

examples.name: The n=1 check
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that C_1(X) is just X, a basic sanity check on the matrix-point construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the n=1 case is used as a sanity check, with the speaker explaining that 1-by-1 matrix points recover the original variety since the matrix equations reduce to scalar equations

passes.reason: The n=1 scalar case is the minimal matrix size and collapses C_1(X) back to the ordinary variety X.

examples.name: Two eigenvalues and an eigenbasis for A^1
examples.note: For X=A^1 and n=2, two scalars lambda_1,lambda_2 and a decomposition into eigenlines are sent to the matrix acting by lambda_i on the i-th line, illustrating the semisimple comparison map.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the map explicitly for x=A^1 and n=2, taking two eigenvalues and a chosen eigenbasis and constructing the matrix with T v_i = lambda_i v_i

examples.name: Noncommuting and q-commuting matrix equations
examples.note: An audience question proposes substituting noncommuting matrices into ordered equations, and the speaker mentions xy=qyx-type matrix pairs as a broader noncommutative variant where analogous point-count product formulas can occur.
examples.engagement_reason: noncommuting ordered substitutions and q-commuting equations are raised only as possible generalizations with nice product formulas, without any concrete computation or construction carried out
